message:
feat(raptorq,runtime): expand erasure recovery tests and quiescence monitoring

- raptorq.c: extend erasure-aware decode with additional recovery paths,
  improve symbol count inference, and harden encode/decode edge cases
- test_raptorq.c: add comprehensive erasure coding test suite covering
  multi-erasure recovery scenarios, boundary conditions, symbol ordering,
  and codec round-trip validation under various loss patterns (+305 lines)
- quiescence.c: expand runtime quiescence detector with improved state
  tracking and notification mechanisms
- test_quiescence.c: add additional quiescence monitoring tests covering
  region lifecycle transitions and drain-to-idle convergence
- Update raptorq and runtime header declarations
- Sync beads issue tracker

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id":"bd-1fsi","title":"RaptorQ: Implement XOR peeling erasure solver","description":"Implement the XOR peeling decoder in asx_raptorq_decode_with_erasures().

## Algorithm
The encoder (raptorq.c lines 82-94) generates repair symbols using:
    repair[j] = XOR of source[i] for all i where ((i + j) % r == 0 || k <= r)

The peeling decoder inverts this:
1. Copy all present source symbols to output buffer
2. Build equation list: for each present repair symbol, record which source symbols it covers
3. Peeling loop (max k iterations):
   a. Scan equations for one with exactly 1 missing source symbol
   b. Recover that source: XOR repair symbol with all known source symbols in the equation
   c. Mark source as recovered, update all equations referencing it
   d. Repeat until no more single-unknown equations
4. If all source symbols recovered: return ASX_OK
5. If stuck (no single-unknown equations but sources still missing): return ASX_E_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED

## Data structures (stack-allocated, no malloc)
- uint8_t source_recovered[k]: per-source recovery status
- Working buffer for intermediate XOR (symbol_size bytes, on stack or in output area)

## Constraints
- Max k = ASX_RAPTORQ_MAX_SOURCE_SYMBOLS (from config, typically ≤256)
- Max r = k * overhead_pct / 100 (bounded by config)
- Must work without heap allocation (embedded target viability)

-{"id":"bd-3nny","title":"RaptorQ: Add decodability rank check (early failure)","description":"Add an early-exit check before the peeling solver runs.

## Check
Count present source symbols (P_s) and present repair symbols (P_r). If P_s + P_r < k (total source count), return ASX_E_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED immediately — there are not enough symbols to even theoretically recover.

Also: if all source symbols are present (P_s == k), skip the solver entirely and memcpy (fast path).

## Integration
This check goes at the top of asx_raptorq_decode_with_erasures(), before the peeling loop.

 {"id": "bd-aw69", "title": "EPIC: Embedded Target Matrix Gate (GATE-EMBEDDED)", "description": "## Goal
Implement GATE-EMBEDDED: cross-compilation for ARM and RISC-V targets with QEMU-based smoke
tests, validating that the C port builds and runs correctly on constrained embedded platforms.

## Why This Matters
The C port's raison d'être is portability to embedded targets that Rust's async runtime cannot
reach (bare-metal MCUs, router-class devices, automotive ECUs). Without actually building and
testing on these targets, the "portable ANSI C" claim is aspirational rather than proven.

GATE-EMBEDDED is one of the 9 non-negotiable quality gates from AGENTS.md:
"Embedded target matrix gate (cross-build + QEMU + device smoke)."

The three resource classes (R1=tight, R2=balanced, R3=roomy) defined in asx_config.h are
specifically designed for embedded deployment. The FREESTANDING and EMBEDDED_ROUTER profiles
compile with no libc dependency. This gate proves those profiles work end-to-end.

## What "Done" Looks Like
- Cross-compilation succeeds for: arm-none-eabi (Cortex-M), aarch64-linux-gnu, riscv32-unknown-elf
- QEMU user-mode runs the C test suite for aarch64 and riscv32 targets
- Bare-metal builds (arm-none-eabi) link successfully with FREESTANDING profile
- Resource class R1 builds fit within 64KB RAM budget
- make embedded-matrix passes in CI

## Architectural Context
The Makefile already has profile-parity targets that build with ASX_PROFILE_FREESTANDING and
ASX_PROFILE_EMBEDDED_ROUTER. The freestanding hooks (src/platform/freestanding/hooks.c) provide
stub implementations for platforms without libc. What's missing is the actual cross-compiler
toolchains, QEMU setup, and the CI integration to exercise them.

## Risk
- Cross-compiler toolchain installation varies by host OS
- QEMU user-mode may not perfectly emulate all target quirks
- Bare-metal linking requires a linker script and startup code
- R1 memory budget (64KB) may require tuning ASX_MAX_* constants", "notes": "## CRITICAL: EXISTING INFRASTRUCTURE AUDIT (2026-03-12)

- tools/ci/run_qemu_smoke.sh: QEMU testing for Linux-musl targets

- tools/ci/generate_layout_budget_report.sh: Binary size/layout reporting
- tools/ci/compute_layout_budget_delta.sh: Cross-run regression detection

## What This Epic ACTUALLY Adds (COMPLEMENTARY, NOT REPLACEMENT)
Bare-metal cross-compilation for FREESTANDING/EMBEDDED_ROUTER profiles on:
- ARM Cortex-M (arm-none-eabi-gcc) — new toolchain
- RISC-V (riscv-elf-gcc) — new toolchain
- QEMU semihosting for bare-metal binaries — new execution mode
- R1 memory budgets for .text/.bss sections — new measurements

 {"id":"bd-c7nt","title":"[Epic] POSIX Platform Adapter","description":"Replace the empty src/platform/posix/hooks.c stub with a real POSIX platform adapter that populates asx_runtime_hooks for live-mode POSIX targets.

## Background
The asx runtime uses a composed hook family architecture (asx_runtime_hooks in include/asx/asx_config.h) with 5 categories: allocator, clock, entropy, reactor, log. Default implementations in src/runtime/hooks.c provide safe fallbacks (malloc, return-0 clock, counter PRNG, 0-event reactor, stderr log). Platform adapters override these defaults with real OS primitives.

Currently src/platform/posix/hooks.c is completely empty inside its #ifdef ASX_PROFILE_POSIX guard. This means anyone building with -DASX_PROFILE_POSIX gets zero platform integration — no real clock, no real entropy, no real reactor, no real threading.

## Scope
Wave B/C. The CORE profile (Wave A) works without this, but POSIX profile users need real hooks for live-mode operation.

## Deliverables
1. Clock hooks: clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) for wall time, passthrough for logical time
2. Entropy hook: getrandom(2) with /dev/urandom fallback for seeded PRNG initialization
3. Reactor hook: epoll_create1/epoll_ctl/epoll_wait wrapper for the reactor.wait_fn
4. Blocking pool worker: pthread_create + condvar replacing inline execution
5. Export function: asx_posix_hooks_install(asx_runtime_hooks *h) as single entry point
6. Public header: include/asx/platform/posix.h declaring the install function
7. Unit tests: clock monotonicity, entropy distribution, reactor basic poll
8. Makefile: build-posix target with conditional PLATFORM_SRC and -lpthread linking
9. E2E smoke: tests/e2e/posix_adapter_smoke.sh exercising clock+entropy+reactor in a live scenario

## Hook signatures (from asx_config.h)
- allocator: malloc_fn(ctx, size), realloc_fn(ctx, ptr, size), free_fn(ctx, ptr)
- clock: now_ns_fn(ctx) -> asx_time, logical_now_ns_fn(ctx) -> asx_time
- entropy: random_u64_fn(ctx) -> uint64_t
- reactor: wait_fn(ctx, timeout_ms, ready_count), ghost_wait_fn(ctx, logical_step, ready_count)
- log: write_fn(ctx, level, message)

## Files
- src/platform/posix/hooks.c (modify: replace empty stub)
- include/asx/platform/posix.h (new: public declaration)
- Makefile (modify: add build-posix target)

-{"id":"bd-cye2","title":"Child regions: Enforce parent-waits-for-children in drain","description":"Modify the drain/finalize path so that a parent region cannot advance past CLOSING while it has live (non-CLOSED) children.

## Current behavior
The drain path in quiescence.c (around line 215) advances regions through CLOSING → DRAINING → FINALIZING → CLOSED. Currently it skips DRAINING ("no children (walking skeleton) — fast path").

## New behavior
Before advancing past CLOSING:
1. Check r->child_count > 0
2. If yes: stay in CLOSING, return ASX_E_PENDING (children must close first)
3. If no: proceed to DRAINING as before

This ensures the parent region lifecycle respects the child region hierarchy. Children must be explicitly closed before parent can finalize.

## API
asx_status asx_region_open_child(asx_region_id parent, asx_region_id *out_child);

## Semantics
1. Validate parent is alive and in OPEN state (via asx_region_slot_lookup + state check)
2. Validate parent's child_count < ASX_MAX_REGION_CHILDREN (return ASX_E_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED if full)
3. Call existing asx_region_open(out_child) to allocate the child slot
4. Set child's parent_id = parent
5. Add child's id to parent's children[] array
6. Increment parent's child_count
7. Return ASX_OK

- ASX_E_INVALID_ARGUMENT: null out_child
- ASX_E_NOT_FOUND: parent not found
- ASX_E_REGION_NOT_OPEN: parent not in OPEN state
- ASX_E_REGION_POISONED: parent is poisoned
- ASX_E_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: child slots full or no region slots available
